narrative:
I'm sorry to deliver this news, but the law school's admission model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on student profiles like yours, estimated a 48% chance you'll fail the bar—high enough to lead to your application's rejection. Your profile shows you ranked in the bottom decile for grades in both your first and third years of school, far below the typical student's mid-range performance around the fifth or sixth decile. Your LSAT score of 31 also sits well under the average of about 37 that successful students usually have. While your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 is very close to the typical 3.21, your lower-middle family income bracket is below the dataset's average level. As a white male planning full-time enrollment, you're in line with the majority of students—white applicants make up over 80%, males about 56%, and full-timers nearly 93%—so those didn't stand out much.

The model's decision was shaped most strongly by your third-year decile rank, which had the biggest influence and pushed the prediction toward expecting bar failure since it was so much lower than average. Next in influence came your LSAT score, again driving the model toward failure due to its below-average level. Your first-year decile rank followed closely, reinforcing that early low performance as a concerning pattern compared to peers. Your family income then played a notable role, with its lower-middle status nudging the outlook downward relative to typical applicants. Your undergraduate GPA had a smaller but still failure-pushing effect, even being near average, while your racial background, gender, and full-time plans had the least sway, all mildly contributing toward the failure prediction in your case.

Overall, these factors together painted a picture riskier than the strong profiles of students who typically pass, like those with higher grades, LSATs in the mid-to-upper 30s, and more stable family resources. To boost your future chances, focus on retaking the LSAT to aim for a score closer to or above 37, as that's a key changeable factor with proven impact. Strengthening study habits or seeking academic support could help demonstrate better performance if you reapply with updated undergrad or grad records. Building bar prep skills through targeted courses might also offset early grade concerns, showing the model you're addressing the risks head-on. Hang in there—this is a data-driven call, but with targeted improvements, you can shift the odds in your favor next time.
